title: Kth Largest Element
code: static int FindKthLargest(int[] nums, int k)

frames.caption: Find the 2nd largest of [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4] with a size-2 MIN-heap. Its root is always the weakest of the current top-2.
frames.note: Counter-intuitively, to keep the k LARGEST values you use a MIN-heap, not a max-heap. A min-heap of size k lets you peek the SMALLEST of your top-k in O(1) at the root, which is exactly the value to evict when a stronger candidate arrives. After scanning all n values, that root is the k-th largest — O(n log k) time, O(k) space.

frames.caption: See 3 (index 0). Heap has 0/2 elements.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: Heap not full (0 < 2) → push 3. Heap = [3], root = 3.
frames.note: While the heap holds fewer than k elements we just push: there is no candidate to evict yet, so every value of the first k is provisionally in the top-k. Each push is an O(log k) sift-up. Capping the heap at k is the whole point — we never store more than k values, so space is O(k), not O(n), and the algorithm streams (it never needs all n in memory at once).

frames.caption: See 2 (index 1). Heap has 1/2 elements.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: Heap not full (1 < 2) → push 2. Heap = [2, 3], root = 2.
frames.note: While the heap holds fewer than k elements we just push: there is no candidate to evict yet, so every value of the first k is provisionally in the top-k. Each push is an O(log k) sift-up. Capping the heap at k is the whole point — we never store more than k values, so space is O(k), not O(n), and the algorithm streams (it never needs all n in memory at once).

frames.caption: See 1 (index 2). Heap has 2/2 elements, root = 2.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: 1 > root 2? No → skip 1: it cannot be one of the 2 largest.
frames.note: If the incoming value is <= the root (the weakest of our top-k), it cannot belong in the k largest — there are already k values at least as big. So we skip it entirely: no heap op, O(1). This is why a size-k heap is cheap when k << n: most stream elements lose to the root and are discarded on sight.

frames.caption: See 5 (index 3). Heap has 2/2 elements, root = 2.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: 5 > root 2 → evict 2, insert 5 (one sift). Heap = [3, 5], root = 3.
frames.note: Once the heap is full, the root is the SMALLEST of the current top-k — the weakest member. A new value can only matter if it is strictly greater than that weakest member: if x > root, x belongs in the top-k and the old root no longer does, so we push x and pop the root in one combined sift (EnqueueDequeue). This is exactly one O(log k) operation per element, giving O(n log k) overall.

frames.caption: See 6 (index 4). Heap has 2/2 elements, root = 3.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: 6 > root 3 → evict 3, insert 6 (one sift). Heap = [5, 6], root = 5.
frames.note: Once the heap is full, the root is the SMALLEST of the current top-k — the weakest member. A new value can only matter if it is strictly greater than that weakest member: if x > root, x belongs in the top-k and the old root no longer does, so we push x and pop the root in one combined sift (EnqueueDequeue). This is exactly one O(log k) operation per element, giving O(n log k) overall.

frames.caption: See 4 (index 5). Heap has 2/2 elements, root = 5.
frames.note: We scan the array once. For each value we ask a single question of the size-k heap: does this value belong in the current top-k? The heap answers in O(log k), so the whole scan is O(n log k) — much better than an O(n log n) full sort when k is small or the input streams in.

frames.caption: 4 > root 5? No → skip 4: it cannot be one of the 2 largest.
frames.note: If the incoming value is <= the root (the weakest of our top-k), it cannot belong in the k largest — there are already k values at least as big. So we skip it entirely: no heap op, O(1). This is why a size-k heap is cheap when k << n: most stream elements lose to the root and are discarded on sight.

frames.caption: Scan done. The size-2 heap holds the 2 largest [5, 6]; its root 5 is the 2nd largest.
frames.note: The heap now contains exactly the k largest elements, and because it is a MIN-heap, its root is the smallest of those k — i.e. the k-th largest overall. We did at most one O(log k) heap op per element, so this is O(n log k) time and O(k) space: the go-to pattern for top-k, k-way merge, and streaming "next best" problems.
